ground_truth.title: Building Bridges: Microsoft's Participation in the Fedora Linux Community
ground_truth.author: bexelbie
ground_truth.main_content:
At Microsoft, we believe that meaningful open source participation is driven by people, not corporations. But companies can - and should - create the conditions that empower individuals to contribute. Over the past year, our Community Linux Engineering team has been doing just that, focusing on Fedora Linux and working closely with the community to improve infrastructure, tooling, and collaboration.

Modernizing Fedora Cloud Image Delivery: One of our most impactful contributions this year has been expanding the availability of Fedora Cloud images across major cloud platforms. We introduced support for publishing images to both the Azure Community Gallery and Google Cloud Platform. We modernized the existing AWS image publishing process by migrating it to a new, OpenShift-hosted automation framework. We partnered with Adam Williamson in Fedora QE to extend this tooling to support container image uploads, replacing fragile shell scripts with a robust, maintainable system.

Enabling Secure Boot on ARM with Sigul: Secure Boot is essential for trusted cloud workloads across architectures. Our current focus includes enabling it on ARM-based systems. Fedora currently signs most artifacts with Sigul, but UEFI applications are handled separately. We're working to enable Sigul-based signing for UEFI applications across architectures. We've stepped in to help modernize Sigul, starting with a Rust-based client and a roadmap to re-architect the code and structure.

Bringing Inspektor Gadget to Fedora: Inspektor Gadget is an eBPF-based toolkit for kernel instrumentation, enabling powerful observability use cases like performance profiling and syscall tracing. The Community Linux Engineering team consulted with the Inspektor Gadget maintainers at Microsoft about putting the project in Fedora. This led to the maintainers natively packaging it for Fedora and assuming ongoing maintenance of the package.

Azure VM Utils: Simplifying Cloud Enablement: To streamline Fedora's compatibility with Azure, we've introduced a package called azure-vm-utils. It consolidates Udev rules and low-level utilities that make Fedora work better on Azure infrastructure, particularly with NVMe devices.

Fedora WSL: A Layer 9 Success: Fedora is now officially available in the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) catalog - a milestone that required both technical and organizational effort. While the engineering work was substantial, the real challenge was navigating the legal and governance landscape.

Looking Ahead: Our roadmap includes replacing Sigul with a modern, maintainable signing infrastructure, expanding participation in Fedora SIGs, improving automated testing using Microsoft's open source LISA framework to validate Fedora images at cloud scale, and enhancing the Fedora-on-Azure experience.

A Call for Collaboration: While contributing patches is a good start, we intend to do much more. We aim to be a deeply involved member of the Fedora community - participating in SIGs, maintaining packages, and listening to feedback. Open source thrives when contributors bring their whole selves to the table. At Microsoft, we're working to ensure our engineers can do just that - by aligning company goals with community value.

Over the past year, our Community Linux Engineering team has been doing just that, focusing on Fedora Linux and working closely with the community to improve infrastructure, tooling, and collaboration.
One of our most impactful contributions this year has been expanding the availability of Fedora Cloud images across major cloud platforms.
We're working to enable Sigul-based signing for UEFI applications across architectures, but Sigul is a complex project with unmaintained dependencies.
Open source thrives when contributors bring their whole selves to the table. At Microsoft, we're working to ensure our engineers can do just that - by aligning company goals with community value.
